does: This paper develops a mechanistic interpretability framework for assuring vision-based aircraft landing systems through neural network decomposition. The authors train a vision transformer for runway keypoint regression on the LARDv2 dataset and decompose its per-patch embeddings into interpretable atoms using K-SVD sparse dictionary learning. They demonstrate that the model's internal representations separate content (task-relevant runway structure) from style (domain-specific appearance variations), and show that the regression head relies almost entirely on contentful representation components rather than stylistic ones. This provides concrete technical evidence that the learned system builds a semantically meaningful situation representation aligned with aviation safety requirements.
connects: This work responds directly to EASA learning-assurance guidance for data-driven aviation systems, which mandates that neural networks demonstrate their own situation representation monitoring—a requirement previously lacking technical solutions for deep learning. The paper builds on mechanistic interpretability research that seeks to understand learned representations through decomposition and visualization methods. It combines sparse dictionary learning approaches from representation analysis with safety-critical system assurance practices, extending recent work on separating spurious from causal features in neural networks. The research also draws on vision transformer interpretability literature and safety engineering's demand for explainable autonomous systems in high-consequence domains.
enables: This assurance framework establishes a replicable pathway for certifying other vision-based autonomous systems in safety-critical aviation contexts, potentially satisfying EASA's regulatory requirements for neural-network-based perception. The content-style separation principle could extend to other sensor modalities and landing scenarios, enabling broader deployment of learning-based systems in regulated aerospace applications. The work points toward mechanistic assurance methods that go beyond black-box performance metrics, potentially informing safety validation approaches for autonomous systems in other critical domains. Future work could generalize this decomposition methodology to multi-sensor systems, dynamic landing conditions, and adversarial robustness verification, establishing foundations for trustworthy autonomous aviation where learned representations can be inspected and monitored at runtime.
